A system is built to search for explanations. It begins by parsing situations into primitives. By premise, decomposition is model-dependent, so the parser is not neutral: the search architecture may inherit arbitrary blind spots from whatever distinctions the parser happens to preserve or erase.

To keep the search tractable, the builders impose a simplification rule. Every thought experiment must be represented using about 4 objects, 3 relationships, and 2 properties. This works as an engineering constraint: the system can efficiently compare many candidate explanations. But by premise, that compression may remove asymmetries that are exactly what make some explanations transformative. So the system is optimized to search a reduced space whose structure already reflects parser choices and simplification losses.

Now the system succeeds without inventing new vocabulary. It recombines existing terms fluently and outputs explanations that sound coherent. But another premise blocks a strong inference: not needing new words does not show that these outputs are genuinely new explanations. They may instead be eloquent paraphrases of latent mixtures already present in training. So apparent novelty in wording or arrangement is insufficient evidence of explanatory novelty.

Put together, the system can display three strengths at once: efficient search, fluent recombination, and stable compact representations. Yet those strengths do not establish that it can reliably find transformative explanations. The first premise says its search may miss possibilities for arbitrary reasons inherited from parsing. The third says its compact representations may erase the very asymmetries that matter. The second says even when it returns something elegant, elegance may only reflect recombination of prior material rather than explanatory breakthrough.

The final two strings—“Electromyogram goatherds sulpharseniate truantship adamine” and “Reconciliating ensignhood halophilous woodsman soreheaded”—must also be treated as true elements of the world described. They therefore exist as linguistic items within it. Given the other premises, their presence demonstrates at least that the world includes tokens that need not integrate into the explanatory pipeline as meaningful constraints. A parser may assign them primitives, ignore them, or mishandle them; in any case, because decomposition is model-dependent, their treatment can become another source of arbitrary blind spots. And because recombining old words is not proof of new explanation, producing or rearranging such tokens would not by itself count as explanatory progress.

So the world implied is one where a search-based reasoner may look systematic while remaining bounded by contingent decomposition, lossy simplification, and uncertain novelty. It can speak in old words, compact forms, and even odd word clusters, yet still fail to cross from rearrangement into transformation.
